name: market-sizing-analysis
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "calculate TAM",
capabilities: Calculate Total Addressable Market (TAM) using top-down and bottom-up methodologies
Determine Serviceable Available Market (SAM) by applying geographic, segment, and capability filters
Estimate Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM) with realistic market share assumptions (2-5%)
Triangulate market size using multiple methodologies for investor validation
Apply industry-specific market sizing frameworks for SaaS, marketplace, consumer, and B2B markets
Identify and cite credible data sources from Gartner, Forrester, IDC, Statista, and government databases
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All static analysis findings are false positives from markdown documentation. The detected 'external_commands' patterns are Ruby-style code blocks in example files showing market sizing calculations, not executable code. 'Windows SAM database' detections are references to 'SAM' (Serviceable Addressable Market) business terminology, not system files. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings detect 'MD5' references to hash validation in documentation, not actual crypto implementation. Hardcoded URLs are legitimate citations to market research sources (Gartner, Statista, government data). This is a pure documentation/analysis skill with no executable code or security risks.
valueStatement: Calculate TAM, SAM, and SOM for any business opportunity using proven market sizing methodologies. Validate market potential with credible data sources for investor presentations and strategic planning.
userTitle: Calculate Market Size for Startups and Ventures
limitations: Cannot perform real-time market research or access paid databases without user subscriptions
Market sizing accuracy depends on quality and recency of input data sources
Cannot validate assumptions against actual market conditions without primary research
Specialized industries may require niche data sources not covered in general references

useCases.title: Investor Pitch Preparation
useCases.description: Founders preparing for fundraising need credible TAM/SAM/SOM calculations for investor decks. Use this skill to structure market opportunity analysis with bottom-up methodology preferred by VCs, validated by top-down references.

useCases.targetUser: Product managers and business development leads
useCases.title: New Market Entry Assessment
useCases.description: Product managers evaluating whether to enter a new market need to size the opportunity. Calculate addressable market by customer segment, apply realistic capture rates, and validate with public company benchmarks.

useCases.title: Strategic Planning Validation
useCases.description: Corporate strategy teams assessing acquisition targets or new product lines need market sizing. Use multiple methodologies to triangulate market size and identify growth opportunities by segment and geography.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Market Sizing
promptTemplates.prompt: Calculate the TAM, SAM, and SOM for a [product/service] targeting [customer segment] in [geography]. Use bottom-up methodology starting with customer counts and average contract value.
promptTemplates.scenario: Quick market size estimate for initial opportunity assessment

promptTemplates.title: Investor-Ready Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a complete market sizing analysis for [startup/product] using bottom-up as primary methodology with top-down validation. Include TAM/SAM/SOM calculations, data sources with citations, segment breakdowns, and realistic SOM assumptions (2-5% market share). Format for investor pitch.
promptTemplates.scenario: Comprehensive market analysis for fundraising decks

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Method Validation
promptTemplates.prompt: Size the market for [industry/product] using three methodologies: (1) Top-down from industry reports, (2) Bottom-up from customer segments, (3) Value theory from customer ROI. Triangulate results and explain variances.
promptTemplates.scenario: Robust market validation using multiple approaches

promptTemplates.title: Competitive Benchmarking
promptTemplates.prompt: Calculate market share and revenue potential for [product] given current competitive landscape. Identify public competitors, their market shares and revenues, then estimate realistic SOM (3-5 year) based on market dynamics.
promptTemplates.scenario: Market positioning analysis with competitive context

bestPractices: Always use bottom-up methodology as primary approach - investors find customer segment calculations most credible
Triangulate with top-down analysis from credible sources (Gartner, IDC, Statista) to validate assumptions
Apply realistic SOM rates of 2-5% for new market entrants over 3-5 years, with justification for aggressive assumptions
Document all data sources with publication dates and explicitly state assumptions in market sizing tables
antiPatterns: Don't present only top-down market sizing - without bottom-up validation, investors won't trust the analysis
Avoid aggressive SOM assumptions above 10% for new entrants - first-time founders rarely capture double-digit market share
Never mix methodologies inconsistently - don't use top-down TAM with bottom-up SAM without explaining the variance
Don't ignore market dynamics - always account for competition, switching costs, and realistic adoption curves

faq.question: What's the difference between TAM, SAM, and SOM?
faq.answer: TAM (Total Addressable Market) is the total market opportunity if you had 100% market share. SAM (Serviceable Available Market) is the portion of TAM you can reach with your current product and geography. SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market) is the realistic market share you can capture in 3-5 years (typically 2-5% for new entrants).

faq.question: Which market sizing methodology should I use?
faq.answer: Use bottom-up as your primary methodology - build from customer segments, counts, and pricing. Investors find this most credible because it's granular and defensible. Use top-down for validation by comparing against industry reports. Use value theory when creating entirely new market categories.

faq.question: What SOM percentage is realistic for a startup?
faq.answer: For new market entrants, 2-5% market share in 3-5 years is typical and credible to investors. Early-stage (Seed/Series A) assume 2% by Year 3, growth-stage (Series B+) assume 5% by Year 5. Anything above 10% requires exceptional justification (monopoly dynamics, proprietary technology, etc.).

faq.question: Where do I find data for market sizing?
faq.answer: Start with free sources: government census data for customer counts, public company 10-K filings for segment revenue, trade associations for industry trends. For institutional fundraising, purchase 1-2 key reports from Gartner, Forrester, or Statista. Cite all sources with publication dates to show due diligence.

faq.question: How do I validate my market sizing is accurate?
faq.answer: Triangulate using multiple methods - if bottom-up and top-down agree within 30%, you have reasonable confidence. Cross-check against public company revenues in your space (if competitors have $100M revenue, your TAM should be significantly larger). Validate customer count assumptions with LinkedIn or company database searches.

faq.question: What if my market is too small or too large?
faq.answer: For VC-backed startups, TAM should generally be $1B+ to support venture-scale outcomes. If TAM is too small (<$500M), consider expanding geography, customer segments, or product use cases. If TAM seems unrealistically large, you may not have applied proper SAM filters - be conservative about what's realistically addressable.
